paper_title: Sediment Core Analysis and Age Modeling

methods.description: Process for obtaining sediment core BSL11 and collecting samples at specific intervals for subsequent analyses (grain-size, geochemistry, radiocarbon dating).

steps.description: Core BSL11 was obtained near the channel in the intertidal zone; the current tidal range is~3.5 m (Figure 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain sediment core BSL11 from the intertidal zone
inputs: Core location (near channel in intertidal zone)

steps.description: samples for grain-size and geochemical analyses were collected every 10 cm
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment samples from core BSL11 at 10 cm intervals for grain-size and geochemical analysis

steps.description: while radiocarbon dating samples were collected at specific depth where plant or shell fragments appear (Table 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment samples containing plant or shell fragments from core BSL11 for radiocarbon dating

methods.description: Determining the age of sediment samples containing organic material (plants or shells) using accelerator mass spectrometry (AMS).

steps.description: Radiocarbon dating of samples was conducted using an accelerator mass spectrometer (AMS) at the Korean Institute of Geoscience and Mineral Resources (KIGAM), Daejeon, Korea.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform radiocarbon dating on sediment samples using AMS

methods.description: Analyzing the distribution of particle sizes in sediment samples using a laser analyzer after pre-treatment to remove organic matter, carbonates, and iron oxides.

steps.description: For grain-size analysis, the samples were treated with 35% H 2 O 2 to decompose organic matter
steps.searchable_summary: Treat sediment samples with H2O2 to decompose organic matter

steps.description: and then boiled in 1 N HCl for 2 h to remove carbonates and iron oxides.
steps.searchable_summary: Treat sediment samples with HCl to remove carbonates and iron oxides

steps.description: Samples were then rinsed three times using distilled water,
steps.searchable_summary: Rinse treated sediment samples with distilled water

steps.description: then treated with an ultrasonic vibrator for 30 s to keep them in suspension and facilitate dispersion.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply ultrasonic vibration to sediment samples for dispersion

steps.description: We then performed grain-size analysis using a Mastersizer 2000 laser analyzer (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform grain size analysis using a laser analyzer

methods.description: Determining the total organic carbon (TOC) and total sulfur (TS) content in sediment samples using a CNS elemental analyzer after pre-treatment to remove carbonates and iron oxides.

steps.description: For TOC and TS analyses, samples were treated with 1 N HCl for 1 h to remove carbonates and iron oxides
steps.searchable_summary: Treat sediment samples with HCl to remove carbonates and iron oxides for TOC/TS analysis

steps.description: then rinsed three times using distilled water.
steps.searchable_summary: Rinse treated sediment samples with distilled water for TOC/TS analysis

steps.description: Approximately 3-5 mg of the treated sample was then loaded into a tin combustion cup.
steps.searchable_summary: Weigh and load treated sediment sample into a combustion cup

steps.description: The TOC and TS were determined using a CNS elemental analyzer (vario Micro cube; Elementar, Langenselbold, Germany).
steps.searchable_summary: Determine TOC and TS content using a CNS elemental analyzer

steps.description: The C/S ratio was calculated using TOC and TS.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate the C/S ratio from TOC and TS data

methods.description: Analyzing the stable carbon isotope composition (δ13C TOC) of HCl-treated sediment samples using an isotope ratio mass spectrometer connected to an elemental analyzer.

steps.description: Stable carbon isotope (δ 13 C TOC ) analyses of HCl-treated samples were analyzed using an isotope ratio mass spectrometer (Isoprime100; G.V. Instruments, Manchester, UK) connected to a CNS elemental analyzer.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze stable carbon isotopes (δ13C TOC) using IRMS connected to a CNS analyzer
inputs: HCl-treated sediment samples (from TOC/TS prep or similar)

steps.description: Results are expressed in delta (δ) notation of the Vienna Pee Dee belemnite standard (VPDB).
steps.searchable_summary: Express stable isotope results in delta notation relative to VPDB standard

steps.description: The standard CH6 (sucrose, δ 13 C = −10.45 ± 0.033 ) was obtained from the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) and has a precision of 0.2 or more for replicate measurements.
steps.searchable_summary: Use standard CH6 (sucrose) for quality control and precision assessment

methods.description: Constructing a depth-age model for the sediment core based on radiocarbon dating results, including data plotting, exclusion of outliers, and application of corrections.

steps.description: Radiocarbon ages are plotted as a depth-age curve ( Figure 3).
steps.searchable_summary: Plot radiocarbon ages against depth as a depth-age curve

steps.description: The age model was generated based on 3960 years at a depth of 2.41 m except for a disturbance layer at a depth of 0-1.2.
steps.searchable_summary: Generate age model based on selected age points and exclude disturbed sections
inputs: Key age points (e.g., 3960 yr at 2.41 m)

steps.description: Several ages at depths of 12.19, 12.22, and 7.05 m, which were clearly disturbed during the drilling process, were excluded from our final results.
steps.searchable_summary: Exclude specific radiocarbon ages identified as outliers or disturbed
inputs: List of ages/depths to exclude (12.19 m, 12.22 m, 7.05 m)

steps.description: Results from shells, based on comparison with plants and shells from the same depth interval in the Yeongsan Estuary in southwestern Korea, were influenced by the marine reservoir effect and were biased by 470 years [4,8]. After applying these corrections, the radiocarbon ages from core BSL11 yielded ages of ca. 3400 cal yr BP at a depth of 1.2 m and ca. 12,000 cal yr BP at a depth of 13.52 m, indicating the entire core was deposited during the Holocene.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply marine reservoir effect correction to shell-based radiocarbon ages

steps.description: In this study, we focused on the middle Holocene, from ca. 8000 to 3000 cal yr BP.
steps.searchable_summary: Define the specific time interval of focus for the study (middle Holocene, 8000-3000 cal yr BP)
inputs: Desired time interval boundaries (8000 cal yr BP, 3000 cal yr BP)
